title: Pinchitos morunos
subtitle: Moorish-spiced pork skewers with cumin, coriander, and saffron
excerpt: Small pork skewers marinated in a spice mix of cumin, coriander, turmeric, paprika, and cinnamon, then grilled over high heat until charred at the edges. The most direct surviving expression of Moorish spicing in Spanish cooking, sold from street stalls across Andalusia.
sourceNotes: Pinchitos morunos (Moorish small skewers) are the direct descendants of the spiced meat kebabs of Moorish Andalusia, adapted after the Reconquista by substituting pork (which was forbidden under Islamic law) for the original lamb or goat. The spice mix (cumin, coriander, turmeric, paprika, cinnamon) reflects the medieval Arabic cooking of Al-Andalus. They are still sold from street stalls and bars across Andalusia and the south of Spain, particularly in Seville, Cádiz, and Málaga.

content.text: The spice mix is the point. Where souvlaki uses oregano and lemon, pinchitos use warm spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, a little cinnamon) that are unmistakably North African in character. The marinade should be made into a paste with olive oil and garlic, not a thin liquid; the paste coats the meat and forms a crust when it hits the grill.

Pork shoulder is the right cut. It has enough fat to stay moist over high heat. Cut the pieces small: about 2 to 3 cm, true to the 'pinchito' (small skewer) format. Larger pieces take longer and the crust burns before the interior cooks. Two hours of marinating is the minimum; overnight gives a deeper flavour.

Combine all the spices with the {{garlic}}, {{olive-oil}}, {{lemon}} juice, and a generous pinch of salt to make a thick paste. Toss the {{pork-shoulder}} cubes in the paste, coating thoroughly. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours. Thread onto metal or soaked wooden skewers, 5 to 6 pieces per skewer.

Heat a barbecue or griddle pan until very hot. Cook the skewers for 10 to 12 minutes, turning every 2 to 3 minutes, until the pork is charred at the edges and cooked through with no pink at the centre. Rest for 5 minutes. Serve with crusty bread and a squeeze of lemon.

items.fix: Keep the pieces to 2 to 3 cm; move to a cooler part of the grill after the initial char; the spices caramelise fast
items.cause: Heat too high and pieces too large
items.symptom: Spice crust has burned black before the pork is cooked

items.fix: Shoulder is the right cut. It has fat; pork is done at 65°C internal, which is earlier than many think; remove as soon as there is no pink
items.cause: Overcooked, or loin used instead of shoulder

items.fix: Use fresh spices and marinate for at least 2 hours, the spices need time to penetrate the meat
items.cause: Spices were old or marinade time too short

items.fix: Soak wooden skewers in water for 30 minutes; brush the grill bars lightly with oil; or use metal skewers, which don't require soaking
items.cause: Skewers not soaked long enough or grill not hot enough
items.symptom: Pork has stuck to wooden skewers

content.text: Pinchitos morunos are sold from zinc counters at roadside stalls and fairground bars across Andalusia. You find them in Jerez, in Cádiz, and in the pueblos blancos of the Sierra de Cádiz. They are eaten standing up, quickly, often with a cold beer or a glass of manzanilla sherry. The food history is unusual. The spice mix comes straight from medieval Moorish cooking. The meat, pork, is the most un-Moorish of all, used as a deliberate marker of Christian identity after the Reconquista. Food history written in a plate of skewers.
